# Feedback For Claude generated by review by codex(GPT-5.4)

The product direction is strong, but the current spec set needs a consistency pass before implementation starts. The main problem is that the MVP is described in multiple conflicting ways, which will lead to avoidable rework.

## Key Feedback

- Clarify the MVP input model. Decide whether MVP supports pasted text only, or supports URLs with an explicit retrieval mechanism.
- Remove any requirement that implies web fetching if MVP truly has no MCP, no retrieval, and no external integrations.
- Split MVP scope from roadmap scope. Keep feedback, personalization, persistence, channel management, and autonomous discovery out of MVP docs if they are post-MVP.
- Update the quickstart so it only shows commands and files that exist in MVP. Remove `/rate-digest` and `data/*` references unless they are actually in scope now.
- Simplify the output contract for MVP. Only require fields the MVP can reliably produce from manual input.
- Make retrieval stats, scheduling metadata, "next digest," and feedback deadlines optional or remove them from the MVP schema.
- Normalize insight, action, and resource count targets across all docs so the team is implementing one consistent expectation.
- Replace subjective success criteria with a measurable rubric for novelty, evidence quality, specificity, and actionability.
- Create a small benchmark set of sample inputs and expected-quality outputs so digest quality can be tested consistently.
- Split `research.md` into "MVP decisions" and "future architecture" so implementers know what is decided versus deferred.
- Clean up document encoding issues and standardize files to UTF-8.
- Do one final consistency pass across `spec.md`, `plan.md`, `research.md`, `quickstart.md`, and the contracts to remove contradictions before implementation starts.

## Highest-Priority Issues

- The MVP says users can provide URLs, but it also says there is no web fetching, no MCP, and no retrieval infrastructure. That gap must be resolved first.
- The docs conflict on whether feedback and persistence are post-MVP or already part of the current implementation.
- The output contract assumes an autonomous scheduled system, which is ahead of the stated MVP.

## Recommended Next Step

Revise the spec pack to define one clean MVP, move future features into roadmap documents, and then validate the revised package against a short implementation checklist before any build work begins.

## Round 2 Feedback

The main MVP correction was made in `spec.md`, but the surrounding documents are still not aligned. The remaining issues are mostly stale sections from the earlier architecture that will still mislead implementation unless they are cleaned up.

### Remaining Findings

- Update the contracts to match the revised MVP. The digest contract still assumes autonomous retrieval, MCP, subagents, feedback, scheduling metadata, and up to 5 insights. The feedback contract is still written as if `/rate-digest` is active now, even though feedback is deferred to a later phase.
- Clean up `quickstart.md`. It still references URLs as input, `data/*` files, `/rate-digest`, and URL-based test commands. A developer following the quickstart would still build the wrong system.
- Remove the stale second half of `research.md`. The top of the file now defines a cleaner MVP, but later sections reintroduce the old architecture: subagents, MCP retrieval, persistence, `/rate-digest`, target counts of 5 insights, and Phase 1 questions that no longer belong in the MVP document.
- Simplify `data-model.md` to describe the MVP only. It still models URL-based content ingestion, external source types, YouTube channels, feedback objects, retrieval metrics, deduplication, broken URLs, and time-window logic. That is future-state modeling, not MVP modeling.
- Tighten `spec.md` a little further. The core flow is improved, but the MVP entity list still includes future concepts like `YouTube Channel`, `Feedback`, and cross-source normalized `Content`. Also, the success criterion "useful to a senior engineer" is still subjective unless tied back to the benchmark/rubric.
- Finish the encoding cleanup. Character corruption is still visible across multiple files, which hurts readability and trust in the spec pack.

### Recommended Next Step For Round 2

- Make `spec.md` the source of truth for MVP.
- Bring `quickstart.md`, both contracts, `research.md`, and `data-model.md` into exact alignment with that MVP.
- Move future-state design into separate roadmap or phase-specific docs so the implementation docs stay unambiguous.

## Round 3 Feedback

The spec pack is much closer now. The remaining issues are smaller, but they still matter because they create ambiguity for implementation and validation.

### Remaining Findings

- Remove the stale second half of `research.md`. The top of the file now defines the MVP correctly, but later sections still reintroduce the old architecture: MCP retrieval, subagents, `/rate-digest`, persistence, and target counts like 5 insights. That makes the document internally contradictory.
- Update `plan.md` to match the revised MVP wording. It still says the MVP takes "URLs or text snippets" and still uses placeholders like `<content_urls_or_text>`, even though the current MVP is text-only.
- Fix the scoring bug in `data-model.md`. The model says scores are in the range `[0,1]`, but the inclusion rule says an insight must score `>= 2` on at least 3 of 4 dimensions. That threshold is impossible with the current scale.
- Align `benchmark.md` with the spec on the what/why/how rule. The spec and output contract say each insight must answer all three, but the benchmark says passing insights only need to answer at least one.
- Clarify the "exact counts" rule across the docs. Right now the docs say counts are exact, but also say the system should output best available content with a quality warning if counts are not met. That is reasonable, but it should be stated explicitly as:
  - normal/high-signal runs aim for the full schema counts
  - low-signal runs may fall below those counts with a warning instead of padding

### Recommended Next Step For Round 3

- Clean `research.md` so Part A is the only MVP implementation guidance.
- Bring `plan.md`, `data-model.md`, and `benchmark.md` into exact rule alignment with `spec.md`.
- Add one explicit statement in the spec and contract explaining how low-signal runs are allowed to violate target counts without being considered failures.

## Round 4 Feedback

The spec pack is now close to implementation-ready. Only a few cleanup items remain.

### Remaining Findings

- `research.md` is still internally contradictory. It now says Part B is future planning only, but the stale pre-fix architecture is still appended after that note. This includes MCP retrieval, subagents, `/rate-digest`, "target: 5 insights," and old open questions. That stale content should be removed or moved to a separate future-design document.
- `plan.md` still has stale wording in a few places. The summary still says the MVP takes "URLs or text snippets," and the deferred complexity table still references per-user feedback YAML even though MVP has no feedback or persistence.
- `digest-output-schema.md` still contradicts itself on low-signal validation. It correctly says low-signal runs may have fewer-than-target counts and that this is not a failure, but later the validation rules and quality gate still require exact counts unconditionally. The contract should explicitly separate:
  - validation for normal/high-signal runs
  - exception behavior for low-signal runs

### Recommended Next Step For Round 4

- Remove the stale future-architecture appendix that is still hanging off the end of `research.md`, or move it into a separate roadmap document.
- Do a final wording cleanup in `plan.md` so every MVP description says text-only input.
- Adjust the output contract so low-signal exceptions do not fail the same validation rules intended for normal runs.

## Round 5 Feedback

The spec pack is now largely coherent and close to implementation-ready. Only one substantive rule mismatch and one minor labeling issue remain.

### Remaining Findings

- Align the low-signal exception rules between `spec.md` and `digest-output-schema.md`. The spec says low-signal runs may be fewer than normal minimums, but the output contract still requires at least 2 anti-patterns and at least 3 resources for low-signal runs. If the principle is "honesty over padding," low-signal runs should be allowed to fall below those normal minimums too.
- Minor labeling cleanup in `plan.md`: it still presents the contracts directory as a Phase 1 deliverable set, even though `feedback-input-schema.md` is explicitly a Phase 3 feature elsewhere. That is not a design problem, but it can confuse implementers who skim the docs tree.

### Recommended Next Step For Round 5

- Make the low-signal rule fully consistent across the spec and output contract:
  - high-signal runs target the full schema counts
  - low-signal runs may publish fewer items in any section, as long as the quality warning is visible and there is no padding
- Optionally relabel the contracts section in `plan.md` so future-phase contracts are clearly marked as reference-only.
